displayTitle: 2026-02-12 - Justice Committee - Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended...
topic: Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended...
description: Thursday 12 February 2026 | Justice Committee Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended Supervision Orders under the Parole Act 2002 Submitter: Law Commission Claudia Geiringer, Commissioner Thomas Buocz, Senior Legal and Policy Adviser Dean Knight The Law Association New Zealand Marie Dyhrberg KC New Zealand Law Society Chris Macklin, Criminal Law Committee convener Monique van Alphen Fyfe, Human Rights and Privacy Committee member
descriptionSummary: Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended.... Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended Supervision...

chunks.text: Have a good for time, but it'd be great. But leave time for of questions if you can. Yeah. Thank you. Um, I'm Claudia Inger. I'm one of the commissioners on the New Zealand Law Commission. Um, we thought given the level of overlap between the chisel judgment and the issues that we canvas in our report, that it was appropriate for us to come and offer what assistance we can to the committee.

Our formal role ends when we submit a report, and we do not advocate for implementation of our work. So I'm not here to advocate, I'm just simply here to provide what assistance I can. In fact, when I say bye, I mean him because I'm gonna hand over to my colleague Thomas Burt, who's going to present the submission and, um, take the lead and answer any questions.

Cool. Good afternoon. Um, from myself. Um, I'll try and be as helpful as I can to the committee by explaining how our recent report on preventive detention, extended supervision orders and public protection orders relates to the findings the Supreme Court made in the Chisel case.

Um, I'll keep my statement brief and take our written submission as read, if that's agreeable to you. Yep. That's, and I'll just provide a little bit of context of our review and then we can jump to questions you may have. Cool. Um, we commenced our review of the Law of Preventive Detention, esos and PPOs after the Court of Appeal had issued its, uh, judgment, um, in 2022.

Um, we then, uh, undertook two rounds of public consultation. First on, um, the issues we had had identified with the current law. Second, with our draft recommendations to address them. And throughout the process, we engaged with stakeholders, experts, academics. We submitted our final report to the Ministers of Justice and Corrections in March last year.

And, um, that was, uh, four months after the Supreme Court released its decision in Chisel. Um, that was an intentional decision to wait for that decision to come out so we could take it into account for our final recommendations. We, as we said in our, um, written submissions are confident that, um, implementing our recommendations would both, uh, meet both address the, uh, human rights inconsistencies the Supreme Court has, um, found and improve public safety.

I'll just say a little bit about what's in the final report. Uh, the main issues we identified are, um, the ongoing human rights challenges. Uh, it with regarding to the sentence of preventive detention, uh, before the UN Human Rights Committee, and regarding esos and PPOs before the domestic courts.

In addition to those human rights challenges, we identified substantial fragmentation issues in how the three relevant statutes, the Sentencing Act, the Parole Act, and the Public Safety, public Protection Orders Act overlap and work together. There were several inconsistencies because of the piecemeal way, how those three statutes had developed over time.

The key recommendation we made in the report was to repeal the current law, replace it with a new act, and that that new act should only provide for post sentence orders, not a sentence like preventive detention. We recommend carrying over several aspects of the existing ESO and PPO regime into the new act, but to improve it in some key areas.

That would, um, be the context I'd provide to you for now, and I'm happy to answer any questions you may have. Okay. We've got lots of lawyers in the room, um, right. Who wants to kick off? We go. No, I'm, I'm happy with that. Okay. Oh yeah. Go. Um, Ms. Walter, Um, thank you.

And thank you for the, uh, the table at the end of your submission, which I found really helpful comparing the chisel recommendations with your as well. Um, one of the decision points in Sal was the reflection on, um, retrospective application. And I didn't see that unless I missed it in the table.

Did you have a recommendation on that and whether there was a way, 'cause the decision was fairly definitive on that front. Um, and then I've just got a second question as well. Shall I answer that one first? Yeah. Um, we did think quite a lot about retrospective application. Um, the details are in chapter 19 of our report, we recommended that other Department of Corrections is best place, best place to make the call on transitional provisions as it has significant resourcing implications.

What we did in our report is step through each scenario, um, of which person is subject to which order, whether it was, um, imposed, retrospectively, or pro prospectively to begin with. And according to the Supreme Court judgment, what can be done in terms of transitional arrangements?

The Supreme Court in Chisel did, um, exhaustively say that any preventive measure involving detention that is imposed retrospectively cannot be justified, which limits the options. Okay. So, so am I correct that you are saying it potentially could be a case by case, though, in terms of assessing the particular order, the particular conditions, but also potentially the particular risk in that case, the, for the transition range for the transitions? Yeah, That's right.

Um, and the second question was, um, you made the point about it being post sentence. You would've seen the Law Society's proposal that you, one option was to do it as a conditional provision attached to sentencing. Um, is, could, could you just speak to the rationale behind excluding that as an option?

Happy, happy to do that? Yes. Um, before reaching our conclusion that, um, our recommendation was going to be an only post sentence order regime, we did step through all alternatives we could conceive of, um, including the one that, um, law Society, um, brought up in their submission.

Our key reason for, um, recommending a post sentence regime is that alternatives that, um, add a mechanism to sentencing do not necessarily fully address the issue of a second punishment. There's still a possibility that the courts would find the subsequent imposition of a measure to be a second punishment despite it originating at the point of sentencing.

Right. The second issue we identified with that approach is, um, if imposing that measure, its sentencing without yet being able to take into account whether the person, the relevant person would go through a rehabilitative journey in prison or the opposite, um, be, um, uh, show signs of a higher re-offending risk, it would not be possible to take those conditions into account at the point of sentencing.

Um, having a preliminary measure imposed that can be later invalidated also risks making the path seem like a fatal contemplate that is geared towards, um, that person later on having that measure imposed. Those were our main reason reasons for, um, preferring the post sentence regime, uh, related and secondary reason is having only one point in time where measures can be imposed mm-hmm.

Is the best setting for the court to be able to assess all available measures in, uh, the full spectrum of restrictiveness in one point in time without having to make speculations what order may be imposed at a later point in time, how the person's risk will develop over time.

Uh, and I think that makes sense. Just outta curiosity. Does, does any other jurisdiction do this in a way that has that sentencing conditional measure imposed, or is the most common way of approaching it post sentencing? There is a range of approaches overseas, and it's actually quite difficult to discern a majority pathway or a, or a best practice example.

Um, England and Wales, Canada and, uh, Scotland, I believe all have, um, imposition of preventive measures at sentencing. Most Australian jurisdictions do it post sentence order, but also at sentencing, we found that none of those were convincing examples to lead us, uh, to develop a best practice example.

They all, um, come with their own issues and human rights litigation. Okay. Cool. Any other questions from any members? Um, just let's not back. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, can we just get down to the basics here? So, we are dealing with situations where we've got victims of very serious offending.

So we wanna make sure these victims are protected from the real high risk of re-offending. And all I'm hearing about is the human rights of these people that have enacted these disgusting, horrible crimes against victims. And I get it, human rights is something that should be protected, but I keep coming back to the victims and what, what we're trying to be convinced that we should change in this matter is extra protection for victims.

Mm-hmm. What do you have to say to that? Please help me through that. Um, I'll just, um, talk to the reasoning we adopted in, in our report. Yep. And that was that the important balancing act, uh, to achieve in this area is between public safety of the community on the one hand, and the liberty rights of those subject to preventive measures.

It is, um, uh, chapter three of our report deals with the very first question, is there a need to have something like preventive measures in place to protect the community? And our conclusion was, yes, that is necessary. Most other, um, all other comparative jurisdictions that we looked at have some form of measure.

And, um, were, were not for that measure. That'd be, um, a risk of a very small group of highly dangerous, um, offenders to re-offend once they're released from a, from a sentence of imprisonment. As I said before, this public safety objective needs to be balanced, um, with the human rights of the individual person subject to a measure.

And what we found in developing our recommendations is that usually the, the measures that best achieve human rights consistency are also the best measures to achieve public safety long term. What I mean by that is, um, emphasizing the need for rehabilitative programs and, um, uh, a careful consideration of how a person, uh, can attend programs and make progress in their rehabilitative journey is also a public safety.

Can I just add a little to that? Just, um, in case it's not clear under the scheme that we proposed all the same, like, types of disposition would still be available all the way through from a community based order to detention in a secure facility to detention in prison if, if people could not be managed in a secure facility.

And one of the things we were trying to do was to ensure that all the measures that we know that corrections find useful to have, are on a very secure legislative footing. Um, so that's another sort of thing underlying the report. And as Thomas said, we were satisfied that by providing for that secure legislative footing and for by increasing opportunities for rehabilitation, we would be improving public safety outcomes.

Okay. Let's store close here because we've got three more submitters. Um, I would suggest some members, let's keep that theme going. Um, it's very useful, but thank you for making the opportunity and actually turning up right now we've got Mr. Mc, uh, Mr. Uh, Knight here. We've seen you before.

Mr. Knight. Yes. Nice to have you back. We've got roughly 10 minutes ago. Thank you, Committee. Um, as you probably know, I'm a professor of Laura, aka Victoria University with specialism within, uh, constitutional law and government law. And I am grateful for the opportunity to address you.

But, um, my submission's a little different because I won't talk about PPOs or esos 'cause I have no particular claim to expertise on that aspect of criminal justice. But I would rather invite you to reflect on the overarching issue of how you as a parliamentary committee should respond to the advice provided by the courts through a declaration as a matter of general principle, political conscience or morality.

And my aim is, is hopefully that we might be able to engender some form of conventional expectation about parliament's posture to the special process of constitutional dialogue, which is, um, enlivened by, uh, these types of declarations. And here, I, in my submission, I pointed to a convention of the peers to be have developed in the uk, which might be valuable, but I think is one that probably doesn't quite fit our constitutional setting.

So my specific recommendation is that the committee, uh, should think about articulating some general principles, speaking to how it might respond within the current constitutional framework covered in standing order processes, but the rights act and the principle parliamentary sovereignty more than simply doubling down on an impugned piece of legislation, but less than blindly or always adopting the court's advice as a matter of formality.

There's a middle ground to be chartered here, and I wanna recognize that that's something that might require a bit of work and conversation beyond the bounds of this, uh, present matter before you in relation to PPOs and the sos. And I'm suggesting you might consider appointing a specialist advisor on the issue, or even commencing a separate inquiry on that general point.

'cause I recognize that conversation's a, a tricky one, but an important one because it's the meta conversation that's in play here. So I, if I illustrate this by, um, uh, sketching some of the principles that I've suggested and, and they're provided only on a very tentative, uh, sketch because the things I'm still reflecting on as this new remedy is finding its feet.

And my suggestion is the principle should be procedural or perhaps better describes deliberative only, not substantially saying that you should necessarily follow the, the court's advice in these circumstances, but thinking about how you go about thinking and grappling with it. And, and those principles should be consistent with the notions of institutional dialogue and deliberation that catalyze what is a really unusual constitutional judicial remedy in play here.

The courts don't have this opportunity very often to speak directly to parliament, and we expect there's a gear shift on parliament's part in response. I'm asking you to think, think about how you might capture that in your deliberation on a case by case basis, while also not ignoring that formally parliament has retained the full power to make laws under the parliamentary.

So, uh, under the principle of parliamentary sovereignty. So the, the, the sketch that I've got are things like consideration of the advice with an open-minded good faith in a way that engages with, in detail with the judicial reasoning. And at least for those first two, that might be able to be taken as a given. But I think it's useful in spelling that out. I suggest the need for reasons and reasons which are logical, principled, and generally proportionate to the gravity of the rights and play, and perhaps reflecting the standard structured

proportionality analysis that is now commonplace in human rights of pay or by judicial bodies, attorneys general, and things like that. But I'm not also wedded to the need to speak in legal lexicon because I recognize that parliament speaks with different voice. I think importantly, there is a need to take care about disagreements about the underlying flax and evidence because you've got the benefit of a advice from a judicial body on these points.

And I'm suggesting you one possibility is there might be a need to accept adequate or receive adequate contrary evidence and make specific factual findings if you wish to disagree on that matter, as is your prerogative. But I think you need to, uh, have an evidential foundation from that. And, um, it may be that that's helped by reports of the law commission, like in this case, which has done the heavy lifting there.

So you can, you can draw on that evidential fa findings if you want to make contrary recommendations or findings, uh, different from the Supreme Court on those factual matters separate from the evaluative matters. I should also add, because of this inquiry in the way that's been constituted, um, I wonder as a matter of principle, whether you might also want to routinely seek open and public submissions on these responses.

My understanding is that these ones have only been done by invitation only to specific groups. And I think when we're dealing with a response and, and, uh, uh, to a declaration in this way, there is a role for the community at large to be able to speak in that conversation, which is anticipated. So, um, uh, they're just some working suggestions.

I'm happy to pick up that conversation on any of those points. But, but just to close with the, the basic points formally there, there are two polemic positions. Parliament and its committees might adopt as a posture when responding to advice from the courts about human rights through a declaration and consistency. On the one hand, parliament can quickly double down and condone the impugned legislation as a matter, as parliamentary sovereignty allows.

On the other hand, parliament could or could be expected to fully hate the advice in the name of protecting human rights. What I'm saying is declarations inconsistency have developed as a middle way through those polar positions where in theory, at least advices offered grappled with and responded to in a meaningful way, sometimes with legitimate disagreement.

With that advice. Important, uh, postscript there, my main suggestion, the C committee should think about spelling out some working expectations about how it should work in practice in that middle space. Good. Good. Now got Dr. Sue, first question. Thank you, chip. Um, thank you so much joining us.

That's really interesting. Um, I have a couple of questions, I guess, um, obviously in terms of the way that the Parliament looks at declaration of inconsistencies is reasonably new. This is only the second case we'll look at. So there hasn't been really a broad pool to draw on, and particularly when you're looking at the processes that's laid out in Appendix F of the standing orders.

I guess my first question is, is this something you have submitted to the Standing Orders review? Say that? Can you just say your question again? Uh, Is this something that, uh, that the submitted that, uh, that Dr. Knight has submitted to the standing orders review? Because that Yeah, I I'm in your hands on, on whether it, where, where it's taken, I submitted on the, the, the, the construction of the process, but not this round of the standing orders review.

I think it's a matter for this committee because in principle, you are the, the, the, the, the, the at the vanguard of this point. And it's appropriate for you as your members, I think, to be thinking in the context of particular declarations. How do I want respond? These responding to useful way to think about that I think is if you're a minority voice on a committee position in relation to response declaration, what would you expect of your majority members in terms of process and deliberation?

Which is the best way to think about how, how is there a expected practice? But yeah, I guess, you know, to that point, one of the things you often say, and in this particular ruling, there were as sending voices by the Supreme Court as well, whether it actually then for any deliberation, maybe by this select committee when it comes to Declaration of Independence, like other sort of non-partisan committees that we see in Parliament, um, you know, reg Regs review, um, petitions, you know, to give a few examples that there should be a different mechanism that is in place
than what is currently being placed in front of a thematic select committee. Um, is that something that is a consideration?

I would say you, this committee is sitting in a slightly different capacity when it's responding your declaration because of the uniqueness of what you get to consider. Yeah, I'm not sure it's a, that you can strip out the politics, for example, out of it, because I don't think that's what human rights jurisprudence requires, because there is a, there is always gonna be a balancing between that, that balance that was described in the previous submission.

And, and, and people will take positions on that as an, as a substantive matter. What I'm inviting you to think about is whether as a matter of processing, engaging in that conversation as a committee and later on a parliament, do you want to, uh, chart some of best practice about how, how to have those conversations.

Mm-hmm. And this is why I don't think it's necessarily best to express it in standing orders and to over redify it with processes. This is a matter of, for you as parliamentarians, as your matter of, you know, civic morality and conscience. How do you want to be thinking, debating, deliberating on, on those fundamental matters? We've got advice from an expert body, externally and un unusual circumstances.

All right. Think we might try that too, coach. But hey, thank you very much for your, um, submission, professor. Thank you. And, uh, please keep going back to the committee. Always very helpful. Good. Right now we're going to the Law Association by Zoom. Is that correct?

Well, hopefully I'm not what I was supposed to be doing. Uh, somebody asked if I wanted, um, to join you as a panelist. I, I'm on the list on behalf of the, uh, the, uh, law Association of New Zealand. So if I've pressed something wrong and I'm interfering with you, please just zoom me out again.

I'm not sure if I'm next in line. Yeah, you are next in line. Oh, Thank you. Here we go. We we're, we, you're speaking to the committee. Oh, good afternoon. Lovely to see you. And thank you. And certainly thank you for the opportunity. Um, we have filed submissions, um, and this is the Parole and Prisoners Rights Committee with the New Zealand Law Association.

So I won't go through all of those because they're, um, they are enough for, for you all to consider and, um, and take on board. And we have summarized what our main points are at paragraph 23. What I would just briefly like to just, um, highlight perhaps are just some of the important, um, things, uh, and just picking up on, um, one of the, um, the, um, speakers is to, uh, we definitely suggest, um, across the board everybody's rights, whoever is, um, affected by these orders is affected by, um, these rules,

uh, have to be protected and have to be considered. We are coming, uh, from a point of view that we would be representing, uh, the, uh, people who are subject to the orders. But that doesn't mean to say we don't consider that, uh, the other parties to these orders, uh, don't have considerations and rights.

I don't don't want them to think that. But what we really would like, um, to con, um, consider is our, our Supreme Court has, uh, made a finding, uh, and quite, quite a, a strong one for any society is that, um, as the, um, PPO and ESO regimes stand at the moment, um, basically as a second penalty that they, that they can't, as they are at the moment, be justified in a free and democratic society.

And that's really our starting point. And we, and we certainly want to clearly protect, um, the rights, uh, and certainly of people who are detained and incarcerated. So, um, we would like, um, you the committee to obviously respect the court's findings, but also there are five main principles, um, to guide your, you know, response and the legislative response.

So that's one, obviously, protection of the community, proportionality and individualization so that any conditions are catered to the threats. Somebody may pose what their, um, background is. There'll be different, um, levels, um, of concern, uh, but to provide, also provide rehabilitation and reintegration that is a good, good ratings and good programs will assist, um, the risk of people going out and committing serious offenses again and creating more victims, which none of us want.

And then just procedural fairness and transparency, and that, that's really what the theme of our submissions are, um, through there. But we do, um, suggest is that it goes back to, um, the legislature that goes back to parliament. It's, um, where we're going to get the courts to try and reinterpret this.

It's, uh, it's not the best way to put in place what Parliament is really wanting and what Parliament says this takes care of everybody's, uh, protection of, uh, their safety and concerns. But, and again, anything, um, at the moment, retrospective application certainly undermines, um, certainty.

So you'll see from, uh, our final, um, wrap up as where our submissions is that we would suggest not. So, um, hopefully we just find or hope that you find our submissions, um, helpful. Um, but again, as I say is that, um, we're not, um, we're representing those, uh, who are the subject of them because that's, um, what our committee does, but that doesn't undermine in any way that you, of course, parliament needs to balance everybody's interest in society.

So we're not, um, we're not undermining that at all, but, but we just feel that if we, our own Supreme Court has found something that is, um, in conflict with our New Zealand Bill of Rights, then, uh, certainly serious consideration and re remedy needs to be certainly considered and undertaking that's a, uh, what we would say is quite a strong statement from our Supreme Court. So Thank you, Mr. Berg.

Now we've got any questions? Um, okay, since This is from Ms. Ley, Um, hello Marie. Good afternoon. Good afternoon. Uh, my question, um, is similar to, to what I posed to the, um, to the, to the law Commission, um, moments ago. I hear what we are all saying.

I, I understand the concerns from a technical, uh, academic basis, uh, and what, what you, um, said earlier, you, you spoke about rehabilitation programs, et cetera, and, um, to mitigate the risks of re-offending. And the way I see it is the status quo is mitigating risks of re-offending.

And for me, I, I just can't get past my extreme passionate desire to protect victims. They've already dealt with so much and to protect them from the real risks of these, um, uh, these offenders that have a high risk of re-offending. And that's why I, I, I just, I feel we are focusing so much on the academia as opposed to the practicalities.

And that's my, what, what what we would think. Um, because our committee, of course has, um, very comprised of very senior members trial, um, quite a number of our have been prosecution, uh, as well as defense. So we come really from dealing hands on with, uh, with people who are really high at risk, um, offenders, what, what, what is best, um, that we can offer to du reduce the risk, uh, of them re-offending.

And, um, I know that some people out there, um, not us, and not, not you, but they read what they see in the media and they seem to think that people just get released, um, very easily. And our experiences, they don't, the parole board is very serious about, uh, releasing people, very high hurdle.

And we have clients that are present for a long time and they have to go through these, uh, programs. But the best way that we see is that if we provide, um, deal with the causes, um, of what they did, and we deal in a really sort of, um, response to pro, um, provide them with all sorts of things.

You know, drug free jobs, um, trauma free, you know, just seeing what happens to the people in, um, the state who were abused and just the, the effect that the trauma has. And it is long term, and we treat, I certainly do, but a lot of us, we treat the parole law, um, as a very tough area of law.

And, um, and people are not granted parole easily either. Now, uh, sadly in any society, you have very high risk offenders, but we also have clients where we know they, they can't be released, you know, that they effectively are on sentences, preventive or life and so forth.

And sad as it may be, they will not ever be released. And so, um, and we have, and as lawyers, certainly defense lawyer, we live with that. We also have high risk, um, offenders, but we try and make sure that we battle all the way through with corrections, um, to make sure that whatever can possibly be utilized for these people.

Um, because if you can mend something, um, that's broken, uh, and and really strictly monitor them, then that definitely reduces the risk. But certainly the, uh, victims are, um, are very well looked after in the parole committee. They, they can speak to the, um, the parole board.

They don't have to, um, tell us what they speak about and they are listened to. And, and we know that that's what we're up against. And we know that our clients have to, uh, really come to grips with how did they end up there and how are they not going to offend again, it's not easily that they are released.

We are just saying is that if that we go too far in incarcerating people under the umbrella of human rights and there's international human rights, then we do have a duty as a, a fair and democratic society to at least look after those people as well, those who are targeted.

So that's, um, that's as much as I can say for there. We, we certainly don't, we certainly don't disregard the place of victims in all of this and, and what they've suffered. No question. But It's sad that some of them will never be released. I, well, I think if we're all, um, honest, you know, I, I do have, um, some clients and I just know they won't be released.

They know, we know it. We, we, we don't, um, we don't identify them, but, but we are realistic and we just know that they, that they're too broken. Okay. And everybody knows. Hey, well thank you for joining us this afternoon. I know you're very busy, but, um, it's been very good.

And, uh, that last, uh, answer was very helpful. Thank you. Thank you. Have a good afternoon. Thank you. And thanks for the Opportunity. Right now we've got, um, Mr. Lan, um, are you on your own? I thought you were going to be joined by someone else, Monique, is that right? Or or are you on your own, Chris? No, um, Ms.

Dan has joined as well. Oh, right. Cool. We got this afternoon. Thank you. Alright, the floor is yours as it were. Uh, thank you, um, to the committee, uh, uh, my name is Monique Mefe and I'm here with Chris Macklin. Um, I'm a member of the human rights, um, and privacy committee for the New Zealand Law Society.

And Chris, uh, is the convener for the Law Society's Criminal Law Committee, uh, will be prevent, uh, presenting the submission in two parts. I will speak very briefly at the beginning about the significance of the declaration. And then Mr. Macklin, I think we'll spend more time speaking about the practical points, um, of where to from here, um, as set out in our written submissions.

Um, the first point I'd like to make is about the importance of the obligations at stake here. And it's, the obvious one is obvi, is that there's domestic human rights legislation that issue our own Bill of Rights Act, but the, the legislation sits within an international law framework as well. Um, human Rights Commission, uh, their submissions to the committee touch on that, those international obligations as well, in particular, the International Covenant for Civil and Political Rights that is relevant to the Declaration.

So New Zealand is subject to a, a Universal Periodic review, uh, before the United Nations Human Rights Council. And it's highly likely that this declaration and the parliament's response to it will be relevant to the Human Rights Council's consideration of New Zealand's human Rights record. Um, so it will have an impact, uh, that the response to it in particular on New Zealand's international reputation for upholding human rights.

Um, and the only other point I wanted to make, which I think touches on some questions that I heard, um, of the, uh, law association just, um, before we came online, uh, and that is that the Law society shares the view of the Law Commission, which I'm sure you heard earlier today, um, that this and of the Supreme Court in, in chisel, that the preventive measures for sexual and violent offending has an important role to play, protecting the community from harm, and the law needs to provide for them.

And the question really is how do we do that whilst also protecting New Zealand rights, um, sorry, human rights and New Zealand's international reputation for protecting human rights, even of, excuse me, our most, um, often our most vulnerable, um, members of society in prison. So the law should continue to provide for them, but the question is how, and for that I will pass over to Chris to, um, continue the Law Society submission.

Yes. Uh, thank you, Monique. And, and, uh, the Law Society arrives, um, at this, uh, particular hearing, standing literally on the shoulders of giants in terms of the academic analysis and the very careful work that's gone before us today. And you've already heard, I'm aware from the Law Commission, uh, you've heard from, uh, the Law Association and of course, professor Dean Knight as well.

And rather than sort of rehash that material, what we sought to focus on in our written submission for you is just a sort of pragmatic step back and look at the way forward. We commend to this committee, the Law Commission's report and conclusions in terms of where to, but even putting that to one side, we identified two main pathways that the committee could recommend for further legislative work.

The first would be to change the sentencing regime to accommodate preventive measures. Um, and the second is to find a post sentencing regime, so respectively called Pathways A and B and our submissions. Um, we noted that there's a lot of work to do to flesh out the details on either regime.

We also note and accept the law commission's findings, that the latter being a post sentence regime change would be preferable. Um, so we keep a boot in each camp, but obviously respect the law commission's view, and note that there's just a lot of work required to really flesh out what would be the key details of whichever pathway that is, that's taken.

Uh, we've supplemented that with some recommendations about interim, so addressing issues with retrospective orders that have been impugned by the chisel analysis, uh, making or suggesting that, um, we find a way to have high court review of existing, uh, such orders and then changes to the, the relevant acts being the Parole Act and the, um, PPO Act to ensure that they are framed explicitly towards least restrictive outcomes.

Now, obviously, that's not a false dichotomy case to case. So when one person hurts another, and these are very serious cases that we're dealing with here, the victim offender relationship, clear. It means preventing future harm. They are going to be released. And so I speak very loosely there, but I just wanted to point out that the idea in a holistic way is to find a balance where the preventive measures both serve to keep existing victims safe, but also prevent future victimization through effective rehabilitation.

And that's where the balance is. No one in this conversation is talking about removing preventive measures. There's academic and organization, consensus, law Association, law Society, law Commission, that preventive measures serve a purpose and are needed. What is required though, is that balance to ensure that they are effective and justified.

And in the broadest sense, the issues with justification can be in large parts, come down to the effectiveness of rehabilitative efforts versus the punitive nature of ongoing restrictions.
